Optimization Objective: Maximize student welfare, satisfaction, nutrition, affordability, and convenience
Key Variables: Nutrition score, meal price, student budget, waiting time, travel distance, menu preference, delivery option
Weighting Factors: w1 nutrition; w2 affordability; w3 convenience; w4 waiting-time reduction; w5 preference fit
Constraints: Budget limit, class schedule, dietary restriction, menu availability, queue capacity
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with vendor profit, waste minimization, and fairness if many students choose the same vendor
Contribution to EVI: Student satisfaction, nutrition, affordability, and mission achievement
Campus Food Example: Students prefer a vendor that is healthy, affordable, and has short queue time

Optimization Objective: Maximize profit, utilization, reputation, and service quality while minimizing waste and penalties
Key Variables: Revenue, cost, production quantity, served meals, leftover, waste, rating, compliance status
Weighting Factors: w1 profit; w2 capacity utilization; w3 reputation; w4 waste penalty; w5 compliance penalty
Constraints: Production capacity, ingredient availability, labor availability, food safety rules, price sensitivity
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with student affordability, nutrition targets, and sustainability if profit dominates
Contribution to EVI: Vendor viability, fulfillment, waste reduction, satisfaction, and ecosystem resilience
Campus Food Example: Vendor balances producing enough meals with avoiding unsold leftovers

Optimization Objective: Maximize supply revenue, reliability, and turnover while minimizing spoilage and logistics cost
Key Variables: Delivered quantity, delivery time, stock level, spoilage, supplier cost, vendor orders
Weighting Factors: w1 revenue; w2 reliability; w3 inventory turnover; w4 spoilage penalty; w5 logistics cost
Constraints: Stock availability, delivery capacity, lead time, perishability, procurement cost
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with vendor cost reduction and sustainability if frequent deliveries increase cost or emissions
Contribution to EVI: Supply reliability, resilience, vendor readiness, and sustainability
Campus Food Example: Supplier chooses delivery timing that supports vendor production while avoiding spoilage

Optimization Objective: Maximize delivery income, route efficiency, and reliability while minimizing delay and environmental cost
Key Variables: Completed deliveries, route distance, delivery delay, rider utilization, delivery fee, emission proxy
Weighting Factors: w1 delivery fee; w2 route efficiency; w3 reliability; w4 delay penalty; w5 emission cost
Constraints: Rider availability, vehicle capacity, route congestion, weather, service radius, order readiness
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with student convenience, vendor readiness, and sustainability if delivery demand is poorly synchronized
Contribution to EVI: Convenience, service reliability, fulfillment, and sustainability
Campus Food Example: Delivery routes are batched to reduce delay and transport burden

Optimization Objective: Maximize campus welfare, institutional mission, sustainability, and vendor viability under budget constraints
Key Variables: Student satisfaction, affordability, nutrition, vendor profit, waste reduction, subsidy budget, EVI
Weighting Factors: w1 student welfare; w2 mission achievement; w3 sustainability; w4 vendor viability; w5 subsidy cost
Constraints: Budget, fairness, governance rules, infrastructure limits, institutional priorities
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with vendor profit if strict rules are imposed, or with budget if subsidies are increased
Contribution to EVI: Mission achievement, affordability, sustainability, fairness, and resilience
Campus Food Example: University adjusts subsidy to preserve affordability during inflation

Optimization Objective: Maximize ecosystem coordination quality, recommendation usefulness, predictive accuracy, and transparency
Key Variables: EVI, acceptance rate, prediction error, policy robustness, dashboard use, compute cost
Weighting Factors: w1 EVI gain; w2 acceptance rate; w3 prediction accuracy; w4 transparency; w5 compute cost
Constraints: Data quality, computing capacity, privacy rules, model validity, stakeholder trust
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with stakeholder autonomy or regulatory expectations if recommendations are opaque
Contribution to EVI: Coordination effectiveness, robustness, learning quality, and evidence-based decision support
Campus Food Example: Operator selects balanced-EVI policy when multi-shock scenario reduces performance

Optimization Objective: Maximize safety, compliance, fairness, trust, and environmental accountability while minimizing enforcement cost
Key Variables: Compliance rate, safety incidents, inspection frequency, fairness score, trust score, environmental violations
Weighting Factors: w1 safety; w2 compliance; w3 fairness; w4 trust; w5 sustainability; w6 enforcement cost
Constraints: Legal authority, inspection capacity, institutional policy, data availability, audit requirements
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with vendor flexibility and cost efficiency when strict compliance thresholds are applied
Contribution to EVI: Safety, trust, legitimacy, fairness, and environmental compliance
Campus Food Example: Regulator prioritizes inspection for vendors with repeated hygiene or waste anomalies

Optimization Objective: Maximize environmental sustainability and reduce negative externalities
Key Variables: Food waste, packaging waste, emission proxy, reusable packaging rate, resource efficiency
Weighting Factors: w1 waste reduction; w2 low emission; w3 reuse; w4 resource efficiency
Constraints: Available sustainable alternatives, cost, behavior adoption, regulatory support
Expected Conflicts: May conflict with affordability, vendor cost, and convenience if green alternatives are expensive
Contribution to EVI: Sustainability score, waste reduction score, and environmental component of mission achievement
Campus Food Example: Plastic ban reduces packaging waste but may increase packaging cost

Utility Function: U_ecosystem(t)=Σ_i α_i*U_i(t), subject to mission, fairness, sustainability, and resilience constraints
Optimization Objective: Maximize coordinated multi-stakeholder value rather than any single stakeholder utility alone
Key Variables: All stakeholder utilities, EVI, mission achievement, robustness, fairness, sustainability
Weighting Factors: α_i stakeholder weights; β_k metric weights in EVI
Constraints: Minimum satisfaction, minimum vendor viability, minimum safety, budget limit, waste threshold
Expected Conflicts: Requires trade-off management among affordability, profit, sustainability, resilience, and compliance
Contribution to EVI: Defines the aggregation logic for ecosystem-level evaluation and MSRS policy comparison
Campus Food Example: Balanced-EVI policy chooses a solution that is not individually optimal for all stakeholders but improves total ecosystem value
